question: What happens if everyone on a team gets a red card?
note: Fixed 2026-07-19 (issue #64): Law 3 › 1 previously never surfaced for this phrasing — a targeted embedding-text override closed the gap (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-19-law3-retrieval-gap-fix-design.md). Retained as a compound question: full answer still needs minimum players, abandoned-match consequence, how winners are determined, and when shoot-outs apply.

question: Can a team keep playing if their goalkeeper is sent off and they have no substitutes left?
note: Needs the minimum-players rule AND the outfield-player-becomes-goalkeeper procedure. KNOWN RESIDUAL LIMITATION (issue #75, 2026-07-23, live-investigated by Opus, not fixed): the model's prose answer is consistently correct and complete, but its citation marker for the 'who plays goalkeeper now' fact consistently lands on Law 10 › 3 (an off-context penalty-shoot-out rule that happens to share the literal phrase 'a goalkeeper who is sent off must be replaced') instead of Law 3 › 4, because Anthropic's native citation markers only attach where the model closely echoes a document's literal wording, and this question's fully-correct answer needs 3 sections (Law 3 › 1, Law 3 › 4, and arguably Law 3 › 6) synthesized into one concise answer that can only literally echo about 2. A targeted prompt fix was tested live and confirmed to only relocate which citation gets dropped, not raise the count — prompt-only hardening cannot close this case. See docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-22-citation-completeness-gap-design.md §7 and issue #90 for the deferred structural fix (a post-generation completeness check) this is evidence for.

question: A defender deliberately punches the ball off the goal line to stop a goal — what happens?
note: Handball direct-free-kick/penalty offence plus the DOGSO sending-off — two sections of Law 12 that retrieval must assemble together.

question: A substitute runs onto the pitch and the ball still ends up in the goal — does the goal count?
note: Extra-person interference rules plus the goal-scored-with-extra-person outcomes table.

question: A penalty kick is awarded in the final seconds of the half — can it still be taken, and can anyone score from the rebound?
note: Time extension for a penalty at the end of a half (Law 7) plus the kick-completion rule (Law 14) that kills the rebound.

question: The ball bursts as it flies toward the goal and still crosses the line — is it a goal and how does play restart?
note: Defective-ball replacement/restart plus the definition of a valid goal.

question: During a penalty shoot-out, can an injured goalkeeper be replaced, and by whom?
note: Shoot-out eligibility rules reference the substitution allowance from Law 3.

question: A defender fouls an attacker who was standing in an offside position but had not yet touched the ball — what does the referee award?
note: Whether an offside offence has even occurred (involvement) vs the foul — the ruling needs both laws.

question: Can a team make an extra substitution in extra time of a cup match, and what is the procedure for making it?
note: Additional-substitution allowance plus the substitution procedure itself.

question: If a referee blows the whistle for a foul at almost the exact same instant the ball is crossing the goal line, does the goal count because the ball technically crossed the line, or does the whistle stopping play take priority no matter how close the timing is?
note: Ball out-of-play triggers the instant the referee stops play, independent of whether it has already crossed the line — retrieval must connect the general out-of-play timing rule with the goal-scored definition, not just find one or the other. Harvested from a real disputed 2026-07-19 incident (see issue #64/#65 comments).

question: Can a referee cancel a free kick they've just awarded, based only on their assistant referee telling them there was no foul — with no VAR involved at all — and how late in the process can that correction still happen?
note: The referee's authority to change a decision based on another match official's advice (Law 5) has to be connected with what assistant referees are actually there to do (Law 6) — a non-VAR mechanism this app's corpus doesn't otherwise call attention to. Harvested from a real disputed 2026-07-19 incident (see issue #64/#65 comments).

question: If a team's players walk off the pitch in protest and refuse to continue for several minutes, what do the Laws of the Game say should happen — do players need the referee's permission to leave the field, can the referee just resume once they return, and does that lost time get added on separately?
note: Combines the leaving-the-field-without-permission caution (Law 12), the re-entry-without-permission procedure (Law 3), the time-lost allowance that covers disciplinary stoppages and time-wasting (Law 7), and the abandoned-match rule (Law 7) — four separate sections for one 'what happens' question. Harvested from a real disputed 2026-07-19 incident (see issue #64/#65 comments).

question: Does the 'you can't touch the ball twice in a row' rule apply the same way to a penalty kick as it does to a regular free kick or goal kick — and if a kicker slips and accidentally double-touches the ball taking a penalty, is the outcome the same as for any other restart?
note: The general second-touch restriction (Law 13) applies to penalty kicks too (Law 14) but is stated separately in each place — retrieval has to find both, not assume the free-kick rule alone answers a penalty-kick question. Harvested from a real disputed 2026-07-19 incident (see issue #64/#65 comments).

question: If, in the same passage of play inside the penalty area, an attacker's arm touches the ball in what looks like a handball, and then a defender immediately fouls that same attacker, which offense governs the restart — a penalty for the foul, or a free kick the other way for the handball?
note: Tests whether retrieval surfaces the simultaneous-offense precedence rule (Law 5) alongside the handball offense definition itself (Law 12) — a real professional refereeing dispute about which infringement 'wins' when two happen in the same passage of play. Harvested from a real disputed 2026-07-19 incident (see issue #64/#65 comments).

question: If the referee allows play to continue under advantage for a foul that would have warranted a red card, does the player still get sent off once the advantage phase ends?
note: Narrowed 2026-07-23 (issue #75 investigation): originally required Law 5 › 3 too, but the question already presupposes what 'advantage' means (it says 'under advantage') — Law 5 › 3 is just the general definition, not something the question is asking about. Law 12 › 4 alone directly and completely answers 'does the player still get sent off' (yes, at the next stoppage). Live-verified: the model's answer citing only Law 12 › 4 is correct and complete, not incomplete. Originally added issue #65 (generation-grounding fix) as a generation-completeness test case, verified against the live corpus 2026-07-20.

question: If a player who is already on a yellow card receives a second caution later in the same match and is sent off, can their team substitute another player in to replace them?
note: Narrowed 2026-07-23 (issue #75 investigation): originally required Law 12 › 4 too, but the question already states as a given premise that the player 'is sent off' — citing the rule that two cautions cause a sending-off just restates what the user already told us. Law 3 › 6 alone directly and completely answers 'can they be replaced' (no). Live-verified: the model's answer citing only Law 3 › 6 is correct and complete, not incomplete. Originally added issue #65 (generation-grounding fix) as a generation-completeness test case, verified against the live corpus 2026-07-20.

question: If a goalkeeper illegally handles a back-pass from a teammate inside their own goal area, is it an indirect free kick, and where exactly on the pitch is that free kick taken from?
note: Genuine two-section question. Law 12 › 2 gives the restart TYPE (an indirect free kick for a goalkeeper handling a deliberate back-pass); WHERE it is taken — the nearest point on the goal area line, because the offence is inside the goal area — lives only in Law 13 › 2. A complete answer needs both facts, from two different laws. Added 2026-07-23 (issue #75); live-verified against the corpus: both sections appear in searchChunks(q,8) and generation cited both on all 3 temperature-0 runs. Constructed plausible scenario grounded in genuine football knowledge, not a specific sourced real incident.

question: Can a substitution be made while the ball is still in play, and what happens if the substitute steps onto the field before the player being replaced has left it?
note: Genuine two-section question. Law 3 › 3 answers whether a substitution can be made in open play (no — the substitute may enter only during a stoppage, at the halfway line, after the replaced player has left and on the referee's signal); the sanction for entering early — caution plus an indirect free kick — is only in Law 3 › 5's 'for any other offences' clause. Added 2026-07-23 (issue #75); live-verified: both sections in the top-8 and both cited on all 3 temperature-0 runs. Constructed plausible scenario, not a specific sourced real incident.

question: A player taking a throw-in lifts one foot off the ground and also releases the ball from behind their head — is that a legal throw-in, and what is the restart if it is illegal?
note: 'Don't over-cite' test (the failure mode behind today's two corrections). The phrasing sounds compound (is it legal AND what is the restart), but BOTH answers live in Law 15 › 1 — the procedure section itself ends with 'If the throw-in is not taken correctly, it is retaken by the opposing team.' Law 15 › 2 covers only OTHER throw-in offences (second touch, handball, opponent distraction), none of which a foul throw is. Added 2026-07-23 (issue #75); live-verified: an initial hypothesis of Law 15 › 1 + Law 15 › 2 was disproven when the model cited only Law 15 › 1 on all 3 temperature-0 runs and was correct and complete — requiring Law 15 › 2 would mark a correct answer wrong. Constructed plausible scenario, not a specific sourced real incident.

question: Can an attacker be caught offside directly from a corner kick, and can they be caught offside directly from a throw-in?
note: 'Don't over-cite' test. The phrasing references two restart types (corner and throw-in), but a single section — Law 11 › 3 ('There is no offside offence if a player receives the ball directly from a goal kick, a throw-in, a corner kick') — answers both at once. Added 2026-07-23 (issue #75); live-verified: model cited only Law 11 › 3 on all 3 temperature-0 runs, correctly answering both halves. Constructed plausible scenario, not a specific sourced real incident.

question: During a penalty shoot-out, do the kicks have to alternate between the two teams, and must every eligible player take a kick before any player can take a second one?
note: 'Don't over-cite' test. Two-part phrasing (do kicks alternate AND must all eligible players kick before anyone goes twice), but both rules sit in the same Law 10 › 3 shoot-out section ('The kicks are taken alternately by the teams' / 'all eligible players must take a kick before any player can take a second kick'). Added 2026-07-23 (issue #75); live-verified: model cited only Law 10 › 3 on all 3 temperature-0 runs. Constructed plausible scenario, not a specific sourced real incident.
